title: Chicken Coop Run for Shed Coop
summary: A framed 2x6/2x4 chicken run with chicken-wire walls and a hinged access door, sized to attach to a shed-style coop.
description:
This run is built as a true framed structure rather than a wire fence on posts — full 2x6 wall framing with 2x4 cross supports every few feet, which is what lets the chicken wire simply staple to something solid on all four sides instead of needing its own separate post-and-wire fence system. That framing also carries the door, which is why the door gets built with pocket holes as its own small, square unit rather than just a wire-covered opening cut into a wall panel.

Cutting the door pieces from a single 10 ft 2x4 rather than an 8 ft board is a real lumber-conservation call worth keeping — three 35" door pieces come out of one 10-footer with very little waste, where an 8 ft board would leave you buying a second one for a single extra piece.

tools.note: Attaches the chicken wire to the frame.

materials.name: Pine, 2x4, 10 ft
materials.note: Cut three 35" door pieces from this one board to conserve lumber.

steps.title: Cut all the parts
steps.body: From the 12 ft 2x6 cut the four 144" long wall runners — each uses a whole board end to end, so cut nothing else from these four and check your measurement twice, because there is no margin. From the 8 ft 2x6 cut the seven 96" wall framing pieces, again one per board with no waste. From the 8 ft 2x4 cut 13 cross supports at 48" and four door rails at 47-1/2". From the 10 ft 2x4 cut three door stiles at 35" — three is all a 120" board yields once you allow for the kerf, and the cut list calls for four, so the fourth 35" stile has to come out of the 8 ft 2x4 stock. Be warned about that stock too: two 48" cross supports need 96-1/8" of a 96" board once you allow 1/8" for the saw, so they do not pair up. Four boards will each take one 48" support plus one 47-1/2" rail, one will take a 48" plus the fourth stile, and the remaining eight 48" supports need a board apiece — 13 8 ft 2x4 in all, against the nine the materials list declares. Square every cut and label each piece as it comes off the saw.

steps.title: Build the two walls
steps.body: Build each long wall as a flat frame on the ground before you stand anything up — a framed wall assembled flat and then raised is the whole reason this run holds its shape where a post-and-wire fence would not. The 96" 2x6 wall framing pieces are the wall's main members and the 48" 2x4 cross supports run between them, spaced evenly down the wall so the chicken wire has something solid to bite into every few feet rather than only at the corners. Note that seven 96" pieces will not divide evenly between two walls, and the source does not say how they split — lay them out on the ground against your own coop wall and decide before you fasten anything, since the odd board most likely becomes a centre post. Join with 2-1/2" pocket screws through the cross-support ends into the framing, and check the diagonals on each wall; every 48" cross support must be the same length, so measure a few against each other first.

steps.title: Attach the tops and ends
steps.body: Run the four 144" 2x6 long wall runners across the tops and ends of the two wall frames to tie the whole thing into one rigid structure — at 12 ft each they set the run's finished length, and they are the pieces that stop the two walls folding toward each other. Get help lifting them; a 12 ft 2x6 is heavy and unwieldy on your own, and dropping one onto a finished wall frame will undo an hour's work. Fasten with 2-1/2" pocket screws into the wall framing, and before you drive the last few, check that the walls stand parallel and exactly 48" apart at both ends and in the middle: the 48" cross supports and the 47-1/2" door rails both depend on that width holding. Set the structure on level ground or on blocks, because a run that racks will never keep a door square.

steps.title: Frame the door opening
steps.body: Choose the bay where you want the access door and frame the opening into the structure with 2x4, fastened with 2-1/2" pocket screws. Size the rough opening from the door you are about to build rather than from a number: the door frame is 47-1/2" across its rails and 35" on its stiles, and you need that plus clearance for the hinge knuckle and a gap all round so it swings without binding. If you would rather not guess, build the door frame first and hold it up to the opening before you fix the framing. Check the opening square in both diagonals — a door hung in a racked opening will catch at one corner no matter how well the door itself is made — and put the hinge side against a solid vertical, not a mid-span cross support.

steps.title: Cover with chicken wire
steps.body: Roll the 36" chicken wire over the top and down the sides of the structure, working from one end and keeping it under light tension so it does not sag between supports. The roll is only 36" wide and the run is both taller and wider than that, so plan your overlaps deliberately: lap each course a couple of inches over the last and staple through both layers, rather than butting the edges where a raccoon can push a nose through the seam. Staple with 1/2" staples every few inches into the 2x6 framing, the 144" runners and every 48" cross support — the cross supports exist precisely so the wire has an anchor mid-span instead of only at the perimeter. Leave the door opening clear, trim the wire back to the framing around it, and fold or staple down any cut ends so nothing sharp is left at chicken or hand height.

steps.title: Build and hang the door
steps.body: Build the door flat on a level surface as its own square unit — that is why it gets pocket holes rather than being a wire-covered hole in a wall panel. Take two 35" stiles and two 47-1/2" rails, drill 2-1/2" pocket holes in the rail ends, clamp the frame flat, and measure both diagonals before you drive a single screw; a door is the one part of this build where out-of-square shows the moment you hang it. Note before you start that the cut list gives four stiles and four rails, which is enough for two door frames, and the materials list carries two gate hinge sets — so decide whether you are building one door or two and check that against the opening you framed. Staple chicken wire across the front face of the finished frame with 1/2" staples and trim it flush. Hang the door on the gate hinges, then swing it through its full arc before you call it done and check it clears at every point, not just closed.

images.alt: Framed chicken run with chicken-wire walls attached to a shed coop
